text: The surgeon navigates the surgical field, revealing necrotic tissue at the top of the gallbladder. The anterior wall of the gallbladder is bluntly detached as the omentum is removed. Stones are visible, some spilling out, and are collected for later retrieval. The pylorus is pulled up onto the gallbladder, and the surgeon attempts to find a plane to separate the stomach from the gallbladder. The anterior wall is off, and the surgeon works to detach the omentum from the gallbladder's anterior wall. The bottom half of the gallbladder is intact but fibrotic, and the surgeon develops a plane to access it. The pylorus is further detached, and the surgeon identifies the common duct and cystic duct junction.

The surgeon attempts to open the gallbladder to locate the cystic duct, removing numerous stones and creating a pile of them. The gallbladder fundus is amputated, revealing more stones. The surgeon tries to trace the lumen to the cystic duct, which is visible in ICG mode, but struggles to find a clear plane between the necrotic gallbladder wall and the liver.

The surgeon attempts to find the lumen of the gallbladder by peeling down its lateral aspect, aiming to access the plane between the gallbladder and the liver. Medial attachments are removed to reach the posterior wall of the gallbladder. A large vessel resembling the cystic artery is identified posteriorly, with no anterior artery visible, possibly due to thrombosis. The surgeon tapers down towards the cystic duct, exposing it by amputating surrounding tissue. Bile is seen flowing from the cystic duct, indicating no distal obstruction. The duct is oversewn with PDS suture, and ICG fluorescence confirms the anatomy, showing the common duct to the patient's left.

The surgeon runs a suture back up to tie it together, removing debris and debulking necrotic tissue from the gallbladder. The top half of the gallbladder's mucosa is cauterized. Compression on the repair shows no bile flow, indicating a successful closure. The surgeon proceeds to cauterize residual mucosa and safely remove remaining tissue.

Reflecting on the case, it just reminds me why I absolutely despise this organ and this operation. I was very surprised to find a necrotic gallbladder that had a drain in it in a patient who otherwise was convalescing and being optimized for cholecystectomy before they developed the cholangitis. I had not seen that necessarily before, so I was surprised. It just reminds us that we always have to be cognizant when we do these cases because it is never just a wall bed. So final look of things. I do go ahead and leave a drain for forty eight hours in this patient. And overall, they've done very well. I hope you enjoyed.
